coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots with tunable emission for multicolor light-emitting diodes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Liuxi
Cao, Xiyue
Jang, Xuanfeng
Zhang, Yuhong
Shang, Bin
Sun, Zhengguang
Zhan, Yuan
coredata.dc:description: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) have highly potential application value in the field of optoelectronic devices due to their preferable stability, excellent optical properties and low cost. Here, the nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (HNCDs) with self-quenching-resistant fluorescence were prepared via a simple solvothermal method with citric acid, urea and 2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA) as raw materials. The structure and optical properties of the HNCDs have been explored in detail by various contrast experiments. The results show that HEMA form the poly(HEMA) to modify on the surface of carbonized core, which can overcome the quenching effect of carbonized core. The nitrogen doping is crucial for the red shift emission of solid-state HNCDs. Furthermore, the HNCDs exhibit concentration-dependent emission and excellent compatibility with silicone sol, which lead to their emission red shifted from blue to red with increasing concentration. The HNCDs were further applied to construct the light-emitting diodes (LEDs), and the multicolor LEDs ranging from blue to red can be prepared by simply varying the type of chips and adjusting the concentration of HNCDs in encapsulating material.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Nitrogen-doped
Solid-state fluorescence
2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate
Multicolor LEDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (HNCDs)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Our work is financially supported by the Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China ( 2021CFB025 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM and b) HRTEM images of HNCDs (inset: lattice fringe images and the size distributions of carbonized cores).
a) FT-IR spectrum, b) XRD spectrum and c) XPS patterns of HNCDs. High-resolution XPS d) N1s, e) C1s, f) O1s spectra of HNCDs.

a) UV-vis absorption (violet line) of HNCDs solution (0.5 mg/mL). The fitting of absorption (imaginary line). b) Excitation and emission spectra of dilute HNCDs solution (0.5 mg/mL). c) Fluorescence emission spectra of HNCDs solution at different concentrations excited at 365 nm. d) Photographs of HNCDs solution with different concentrations (the top of daylight and the bottom of UV light). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

a-c) Excitation-emission matrix of HNCDs solution a) 0.5mg/mL, b) 15mg/mL, c) 30mg/mL. d) The absorption, PL emission spectra of HNCDs solution (0.5mg/mL). e) Fluorescence decay curves of HNCDs solution and solid-state HNCDs (Excitation wavelength is 375nm, the emission peaks are located at 446nm and 545nm, respectively). f) Fluorescence decay curves of HNCDs solution with different concentration (Excitation wavelength is 375nm for 0.5mg/mL, 420nm for 5mg/mL, 485nm for others, the emission peaks are located at 446nm, 516nm, 570nm, 574nm and 586nm, respectively). g) Different luminescent center schematics.

a) Photographs of solid-state HNCDs prepared with different amount of HEMA under daylight (top) and UV light of 365nm (bottom). b) SSF spectra of solid-state HNCDs prepared with different amount of HEMA. The PL intensity of the HNCDs composites with different HNCDs concentration and solid-state HNCDs c) under UV light (λ = 365nm and 1W/cm2) at 30°C treated from 0 to 70 min and d) place in an atmospheric oven at 100°C from 0 to 70min.

a) Schematic diagram of multicolored LEDs with single HNCDs preparation. b) CIE coordinates of blue (A), green (B), yellow (C), orange (D), red (E), and white (F) LEDs. c-h) Fluorescence spectra of (c) blue, (d) green, (e) yellow, (f) orange, (g) red and (h) white LEDs and their working picture. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The illustration of the formation of HNCDs and HNCDs composites.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots with tunable emission for multicolor light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials
Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Textile Materials and Advanced Processing Technologies, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430200, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Textile Materials and Advanced Processing Technologies
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Textile Materials and Advanced Processing Technologies, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430200, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (HNCDs) with solid-state emission were prepared via a simple solvothermal method. The HNCDs show concentration-dependent emission, which lead to their emission both in solution and solid-state can be red shifted from blue to red as their concentration increase. The HNCDs exhibit the excellent compatibility with silicone sol prepared by aminopropyltriethoxysilane, and their composite display the good stability. The HNCDs composites were further used to fabricate the LEDs with the emission color ranging from blue to red just by simply varying the type of chips and regulating the content of HNCDs in silicone sol, showing a great potential in photoelectric device application.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) have highly potential application value in the field of optoelectronic devices due to their preferable stability, excellent optical properties and low cost. Here, the nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (HNCDs) with self-quenching-resistant fluorescence were prepared via a simple solvothermal method with citric acid, urea and 2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA) as raw materials. The structure and optical properties of the HNCDs have been explored in detail by various contrast experiments. The results show that HEMA form the poly(HEMA) to modify on the surface of carbonized core, which can overcome the quenching effect of carbonized core. The nitrogen doping is crucial for the red shift emission of solid-state HNCDs. Furthermore, the HNCDs exhibit concentration-dependent emission and excellent compatibility with silicone sol, which lead to their emission red shifted from blue to red with increasing concentration. The HNCDs were further applied to construct the light-emitting diodes (LEDs), and the multicolor LEDs ranging from blue to red can be prepared by simply varying the type of chips and adjusting the concentration of HNCDs in encapsulating material.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Light-emitting diodes (LEDs) have been the mainstay of display technology and lighting due to their fast response, high energy saving, small size, and long service life [1–3]. Currently, the photoluminescent materials used in LEDs fields mainly include rare earth-based nanoparticles [4,5], perovskite quantum dots [6,7], semiconductor quantum dots [8,9], and organic dye [10], etc. However, the application of these materials in LEDs is restricted by non-renewable, complex synthesis process and toxicity [11–13]. To develop novel fluorescent materials is an effective strategy to solve those problems.

Carbon dots (CDs), as novel carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, have various advantages such as low toxicity [14,15], abundant raw material sources [16,17], excellent photo-stability [18], and environmentally friendly [19], which have become attractive fluorescent materials for LEDs [2,20,21]. However, most CDs suffer from aggregation-induced luminescence quenching at high concentration due to the poor compatibility with encapsulating material, which fail to meet the requirements of high light power output in LEDs applications due to the very low CDs concentration (<2 wt%) [22,23]. To work out this issue, some approaches such as chemically crosslinking CDs with matrix [18,24,25] or modifying the surface of CDs [26] have been explored to improve the solid-state photoluminescence (PL) property of CDs. Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), with an interesting structure of a highly carbonized core and polymer shell, not only can improve compatibility with encapsulating material [27,28], but also can adjust the spacing of the carbonized core to achieve various optical properties due to the abundant polymer chains modified on the surface of carbonized core [29,30]. Furthermore, the fluorescence of these carbonized core can be enhanced by utilizing the chemical crosslinking of polymer chains or physical immobilization, which is defined as the crosslink enhanced emission (CEE) effect [31–33]. For example, Lu et al. prepared two types of CPDs using peel and sarcocarp of avocado, respectively and mixed them with epoxy resin to obtain blue, green and white fluorescent LEDs [34]. Yang et al. prepared strong blue-green fluorescent CPDs in solid state from o-aminobenzenethiol and thiosalicylic, which was successfully utilized to fabricate blue-green light LEDs by mixing them with polyvinyl chloride [35]. However, most CPDs display no solid-state fluorescence on their own, and they still encounter aggregation-induced luminescence quenching even though they can disperse evenly at high concentrations, which hinders their development and application.

In this work, nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (HNCDs) with controllable solid-state fluorescence (SSF) were prepared via a simple solvothermal method using citric acid, urea, and 2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate reacting in ethanol solution. The HNCDs not only show green emission in solid-state, but also exhibit concentration-dependent emission, which makes it possible to achieve the full-spectrum emission by adjusting their concentration. Furthermore, the HNCDs show excellent compatibility with silicone sol prepared by aminopropyltriethoxysilane, and HNCDs-silica gel composites with different HNCDs content can be easily obtained. The obtained HNCDs composite display good stability and controllable SSF, which are further used to fabricate the LEDs with the emission color ranging from blue to red just by simply varying the type of chips and regulating the content of HNCDs in silicone sol.

Citric acid (CA, ≥99.5%), urea (≥99.5%), 2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA, ≥96%, 250 ppm MEHQ stabilizer), aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTS, 98%) were purchased by Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Petroleum ether (60–90 °C) was bought from Tianjin Fuyu company. Ethanol (EtOH, ≥99.7%) was provided from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Deionized water (DI) was used throughout the study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (HNCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: 2.81 mmol of CA, 17.98 mmol of urea and varying amount of HEMA (8.24–65.96 mmol) were added into 20 mL of EtOH, and the mixtures were then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (30 mL). The autoclave was placed into a constant temperature oven at 180 °C for 12 h. After natural cooling, the brown liquid was first centrifuged twice at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove the solid. The supernatant was collected and precipitated with petroleum ether for purification until the upper liquid is no obvious fluorescence emission. The precipitate was added dropwise to a petri dish and placed in a vacuum oven at 40 °C for 24 h to obtain brown solid-state HNCDs.

Some comparative experiments have been carried out under the same reaction conditions, for example, using the CA (2.81 mmol) and urea (17.98 mmol) to prepare the NCDs, only HEMA (57.71 mmol) as raw materials to prepare the H-CPDs, the CA (2.81 mmol) and HEMA (57.71 mmol) as raw materials to obtain the CH-CPDs, the urea (17.98 mmol) and HEMA (57.71 mmol) as raw materials to prepare the UH-CPDs. The H-CPDs, CH-CPDs and UH-CPDs obtained by vacuum drying are transparent gels, while the NCDs are black powder after vacuum drying.

The silicone sol used as encapsulating material was made by stirring APTS, ultrapure water and EtOH with the ratio of 10:7:3 at room temperature for 1 day. The HNCDs composite prepared with 1 g silicone sol and varying amounts of HNCDs is then used as a phosphor to directly drip onto the LEDs chip. The LEDs chip with the HNCDs composite was then kept in a 60 °C oven for 3 h to obtain different color emissive LEDs. Blue LEDs and green LEDs were prepared by mixing 0.0050 g and 0.0526 g of HNCDs with 1 g of silicone sol on the 365 nm UV chip, respectively. Yellow LEDs, orange LEDs, and red LEDs were prepared by mixing 0.1111 g, 0.3333 g, and 0.8182 g of HNCDs with 1 g of silicone sol on the 395 nm UV chip, respectively. White LEDs were prepared by mixing 0.0526 g of HNCDs with 1 g of silicone sol on 450 nm chips.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded by FT300 (FT300, Pico-quant). Fluorescence lifetime curves and absolute PLQY were performed by an FT300 equipped with a 375 nm laser and an integrating sphere. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis, Shimadzu) absorption spectrum was measured with a Hitachi UV-3600 UV–vis spectrophotometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 ADVANCE, Bruker) pattern was measured with Cu-Kα1 radiation (λ = 1.540598 Å). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was implemented with a Thermo Fisher Nicolet iS50 spectrometer using KBr pellets. The 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra were recorded with a Bruker Avance 400 MHz nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer at room temperature. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were measured on a Japan-JEOL-JEM 2011Plus microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was investigated by an Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a mono X-ray source Al Kα excitation (1486.6 eV).

The HNCDs were prepared by one-pot solvothermal treatment with CA (2.81 mmol), urea (17.98 mmol) and HEMA (57.71 mmol) as raw materials in ethanol for 12 h. The formation process of HNCDs is shown in Scheme 1 , in which the CA and urea act as the carbon source and nitrogen source, respectively, and they react to form the carbonized core [29,36]. The HEMA has the –COO, –OH and C=C bond activated by a carbonyl group, which not only can react with carbonized core (1,4 Michael reaction between HEMA and urea), but also can polymerize of C=C under high temperature and high pressure [37,38]. The silicone sol perpared by APTS with many active NH2 and –OH groups is selected as packaging material, which has good compatibility with the HNCDs due to the hydrogen-bond interaction in the application of LEDs [39,40].

The TEM and HRTEM images (Fig. 1 ) show the morphologies of the as-prepared HNCDs. A number of random polymer particles can be observed in Fig. 1a. In spite of the very low contrast, especially for CPDs with low carbonization due to the presence of carbon in the TEM grid, the HNCDs could be determined with the average diameter of 5.24 nm. Some carbonized cores show lattice fringe with lattice spacings of 0.24 nm agreed with (110) planes of the graphite, while some are amorphous (Fig. 1b) [16,41]. There are some non-crystalline region that surrounds the crystalline part, which reveals carbonized cores are possibly wrapped with poly(HEMA) [29,42]. The poly(HEMA) is hard to absolute dehydrate and carbonize, leading to a large amount of poly(HEMA) remaining on the surface of carbonized core.

The surface functional groups of HNCDs were identified by FT-IR spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectra of HEMA monomers, H-CPDs, CH-CPDs, UH-CPDs, and HNCDs are shown in Fig. 2 a and S1. There are strong stretching vibrations at 3000–2700 cm−1 attributed to the large amounts of C–H [43], while NCDs have very weak stretching vibrations in this range, which means that the strong stretching vibrations of C–H in HNCDs are primarily from the poly(HEMA). In addition, HEMA monomer contains a large amount of C=C (strong stretching vibration at 1635 cm−1) [11,44], while the stretching vibration of H-CPDs at 1635 cm−1 almost disappears, which indicates that HEMA monomers are transformed into poly(HEMA) under high temperature and pressure treatment. For HNCDs and NCDs, there is a stronger stretching vibration at 1635 cm−1 than that of H-CPDs, which can be attributed to C=C in carbonized core formed by dehydration carbonization of CA. In addition, the XRD pattern displays the characteristic peak of typical CPDs centered around 2θ = 20°, which is attributed to polymer chains and highly disordered carbon (Fig. 2b). The main graphite diffraction peaks of HNCDs are difficult to distinguish due to the influence of these strong peaks, further proving that graphitizing core covered by a large number of non-graphitized structures [11,34,45]. The 1H NMR of NCDs, HNCDs and H-CPDs were characterized as shown in Fig. S2, the chemical shift peaks located at 2.5 ppm and 3.33 ppm are the solvent peak and water peak. In the 1H NMR spectrum of NCDs and HNCDs (Fig. S2a and S2c), the chemical shift peak near at 5.4 ppm can be attributed to –NH2 from urea. The 1H NMR of H-CPDs and HNCDs are shown in Fig. S2b and S2c, the chemical shift peaks near at 3.5 and 4.8 ppm are mainly attributed to –CH2- in the backbone and –OH from poly(HEMA), respectively. In addition, there is a new chemical shift peak near at 9.91 ppm at 1H NMR spectrum of HNCDs mainly attributed to the hydrogen on N produced by the addition of the amino group to the vinyl group, which demonstrates that poly(HEMA) is linked with carbonized core by chemical bonding.

XPS analysis were further investigated to explore the surface groups of HNCDs (Fig. 2c). The full scan XPS analysis reveals the presence of carbon (63.45%), nitrogen (5.02%) and oxygen (31.54%). The N1s band has two component peaks (Fig. 2d), corresponding to pyridine N (398.9 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.1 eV), respectively. The pyrrolic N may be associated with fluorophore molecules [46,47]. The C1s band has three component peaks (Fig. 2e), which are attributed to the C–C/C=C (sp2 carbon, 284.1 eV), C-O/C-N (sp3 carbon, 286.3 eV), and C=N/C=O (carbonyl group, 288.0 eV), respectively [30,44]. The strong signals of C=N/C=O and C-O/C-N indicate the presence of species with high heteroatomic content, such as fluorophore molecules. The O1s band has two component peaks (Fig. 2f), corresponded to C=O (531.3 eV) and C-OH/C-O-C (532.6 eV), respectively [11,34]. The above results indicate that HNCDs are modified with the abundant poly(HEMA) on the surface and also realized the nitrogen doping.

The optical properties of HNCDs were characterized in detail. There are three strong absorption bands in the ultraviolet (UV) region with a tail extending to the visible region in the absorption spectra of HNCDs dilute solution (Fig. 3 a). The fate of absorbed energy was studied by curve fitting. It can be seen that it has four typical features in the absorption of HNCDs, which are at 220 nm, 290 nm, 347 nm and 430 nm, respectively. The absorption peaks at 220 nm and 290 nm are attributed to the n-σ* transitions (C-OH) and π-π* transitions of the aromatic sp2 (C=C) domains, respectively [11,12]. The absorption peak at 347 nm is attributed to the n-π* (C=O and C=N) of carbonyl [48–50]. Broad tail extending from 430 to 550 nm in the visible part may originate from other functionalized surface groups of HNCDs [50,51]. The dilute solution of HNCDs (0.5 mg/mL) exhibits strong blue fluorescence (453 nm) under 380 nm UV excitation with the PLQY of 23% (Fig. 3b). It is interesting that the emissions peak of the HNCDs solution red shifts from blue (453 nm) to red (635 nm) as the concentration increase from 0.5 to 30 mg/mL under 365 nm excitation (Fig. 3c), which correspond to their fluorescent photographs under 365 nm UV light showing in Fig. 3d. In Fig. S3, the emission spectra and excitation spectra of dilute solutions of HNCDs (0.05 mg/mL) in both protonic and nonprotonic solvents show almost no change, which indicated that the fluorescence of carbonized core in HNCDs is blue. Furthermore, the emission spectra of HNCDs display distinct red shift from blue to red with increasing concentration, which indicated that the aggregation behavior of HNCDs has great influence on the red shift emission due to the resonance energy transfer (RET) or/and reabsorption effect of HNCDs [25,29,52].

In order to investigate the origin of the red shift emission of HNCDs, the emission behavior of the HNCDs in ethanol with different concentration were first investigated (Fig. 4 ). At low concentration (0.5 mg/mL), the emission peaks present a red shift from 425 nm to 575 nm as the excitation wavelengths increases from 300 nm to 520 nm, which indicates it displays excitation dependence emission (Fig. 4a). As the concentration increase to 15 mg/mL, the emission peak slightly shifts from 560 nm to 580 nm as the excitation wavelength increase from 320 nm to 540 nm, which illustrates that their emission is slightly dependent on the excitation (Fig. 4b). As the concentration of HNCDs further increases to 30 mg/mL, the position of the emission peak does not change with the excitation wavelength, showing excitation independent emission (Fig. 4c). The above results indicate that the ethanol solutions of HNCDs with different concentrations have different luminescent centers [25,53]. In addition, the absorption and emission spectra of HNCDs at low concentrations show a large overlap (Fig. 4d). According to the above phenomenon, it demonstrates the occurrence of RET between HNCDs, which often happens in small inter particle distance within R0, leading to the obvious red shift emission in the aggregate state (especially, solid-state) [25,29,52]. The PL lifetime of HNCDs solution is 10.42 ns, which is much longer than that of solid-state HNCDs (4.43 ns) (Fig. 4e), which also suggests that radiative transition competes with RET and then shortens the lifetime [30,54]. However, as the concentration of HNCDs in EtOH gradually increase, the PL lifetime first decreases from 10.42 ns to 6.33 ns, and then increases from 6.48 ns to 8.09 ns, indicating that the reabsorption effect also happens at high concentration, together with RET effect to induce emission red shift of HNCDs (Fig. 4f) and their schematic illustration are given in Fig. 4g [26,30].

In addition, a series of HNCDs were prepared by adjusting the amount of HEMA. Fig. 5 a shows the solid-state HNCDs prepared by various amount of HEMA under the excitation of 365 nm UV light. The HNCDs are brown gelatinous under daylight, and they emit stronger green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light as the amount of HEMA increase from 8.24 to 65.96 mmol. Fig. 5b shows their SSF spectra at 365 nm excitation. It can be seen that the emission peaks are located at 545 nm, and the emission intensity increase as the amount of HEMA increase from 8.24 to 57.71 mmol and then decrease from 57.71 to 65.96 mmol. So, the solid-state HNCDs prepared here has the optimal addition of HEMA of 57.71 mmol with the PLQY of 5.4%. To further verify the effect of polymer chains on the surface of HNCDs and nitrogen doping on their optical properties, the NCDs, H-CPDs, CH-CPDs and UH-CPDs were further prepared (Fig. S4a). Except for NCDs, other CPDs prepared with HEMA all behave as gelatinous substances at room temperature and emit blue fluorescence, which indicates that the polymer chains can overcoming aggregation-induced luminescence quenching (Fig. S4b). Notably, HNCDs undergo a significant red shift (from 430 nm to 545 nm) compared to CH-CPDs, which is attributed to the nitrogen doping induced fluorescent red shift. The above experiment indicate that HEMA and nitrogen doping are crucial for the solid-state and red shift emission of HNCDs.

Since the HNCDs prepared here exhibit excellent concentration-dependent emission, so the HNCDs composites varied with different concentrations of HNCDs were first prepared by mixing the silicone sol and the HNCDs. Photographs (Fig. S5a) show these HNCDs composites get darker under daylight and experience gradual decline in transparency when the loading fraction of HNCDs increases from 0.5 to 45 wt% in the silicone sol, which corresponds to their transmission spectra (Fig. S5b). In addition, the HNCDs composites exhibit no any agglomeration leading to the film unevenness, indicating that HNCDs are well compatible with silicone sol. The stability of HNCDs composites were also measured. At ambient room temperature, a UV light (λ = 365 nm and 1 W/cm2) was used to treat the HNCDs composites and solid-state HNCDs. As is shown in Fig. 5c and Table S1, the PL intensity of the HNCDs composites is maintained above 90% after 70 min except for the sample with the lowest concentration of 0.5 wt% is 83%. Meanwhile, the solid-state HNCDs also have higher photo-stability (86%) than that of most organic dyes [54]. Moreover, the temperature of LEDs under the operating voltage is approximately 85 °C [41,55], so the HNCDs composites with different concentration of HNCDs were also treated at 100 °C for 70 min to explore their thermostability (Fig. 5d and Table S2). After 70 min of heating, the PL intensity of the HNCDs composites increases first as the concentration of HNCDs in the silicone sol increases from 0.5 to 10 wt%, and then decrease as the concentration further increase from 10 to 45 wt%. The PL intensity of all HNCDs composites can retain about 80%. The results show that the composite formed by silica sol and HNCDs can improve the stability, indicating the great potential for the application of HNCDs composites as a color conversion layers in LEDs.

Considering that the HNCDs composites have controllable SSF and excellent stability, the HNCDs composites were further used as the color conversion material for manufacturing LEDs. Currently, multicolor LEDs involve various kinds of fluorescent materials with different optical properties, which often require tedious steps, including different preparation methods, multiple purification techniques, and exploring good compatibility packaging materials [30,56]. Therefore, the development of single phosphor-based LEDs has significant advantages in those respects. The HNCDs composites with the HNCDs concentration of 0.5 wt%, 5 wt%, 10 wt%, 25 wt% and 45 wt% were dropped onto the LEDs chips with emission wavelength of 365, 365, 395, 395, and 395 nm, respectively, and then cured at 60 °C for 3 h to make LEDs with different emission (Fig. 6 a). The PLQY of the HNCDs composites with concentrations of 0.5 wt%, 5 wt%, 10 wt%, 25 wt% and 45 wt% were 21.8%, 16.1%, 10.6%, 7.3% and 5.9%, respectively. Fig. 6c-g show the emission spectra of the obtained LEDs. It can be seen that the main emission peaks are at about 464, 535, 577, 635 and 656 nm, respectively, which match their working pictures. These LEDs are characterized with Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of A (0.197, 0.246), B (0.308, 0.411), C (0.506, 0.488), D (0.574, 0.423), and E (0.681, 0.317) (Fig. 6b), which correspond to blue LEDs, green LEDs, yellow LEDs, orange LEDs, and red LEDs, respectively. In particular, the yellow LEDs, orange LEDs and red LEDs show high color saturation. In addition, the HNCDs composites with the concentration of HNCDs of 5 wt% can further applied to construct the white LEDs with the blue chip of 450 nm (Fig. 6h). The resulting white LEDs shows the CIE is at F (0.294, 0.264), the color rendering index (CRI) is 71.7, which is near to that of the commercial white LEDs (65–80) [57]. In general, the regulation method of solid-state emission of CPDs is relatively simple and the emission color of LEDs prepared by CPDs is relatively abundant in this work comparing to other similar works (Table S3). The multicolor LEDs from blue to white emission can be realized by simply varying the type of chips and regulating the content of solid-state HNCDs.

In conclusion, the HNCDs were prepared via a simple solvothermal method, where the abundant poly(HEMA) on the surface of carbonized core can prevent their aggregation quenching effect to achieve the SSF. Furthermore, the HNCDs exhibit excellent compatibility with silicone sol, which lead to their emission red shifted from blue to red with increasing concentration from 0.5 to 45 wt%. The HNCDs composites exhibit photo-stability and thermostability after continuous treated with UV irradiations or heating at 100 °C for 70 min. The HNCDs composites are further used as the color conversion material for application of LEDs, and multicolor LEDs from blue to red are obtained just by varying the type of chips and the content of HNCDs, which provides a new method towards low cost and highly performance multicolor LEDs.

Liuxi Zhao: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Xiyue Cao: Investigation. Xuanfeng Jang: Formal analysis. Yuhong Zhang: Investigation. Bin Shang: Investigation. Zhengguang Sun: Supervision. Yuan Zhan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Our work is financially supported by the Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2021CFB025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122815.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ethanol-derived white emissive carbon dots: the formation process investigation and multi-color/white LEDs preparation
Nano Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Wang, H. Song, Z. Tang, B. Yang, S. Lu, Ethanol-derived white emissive carbon dots: the formation process investigation and multi-color/white LEDs preparation, Nano Res. 15 (2) (2022) 942-949. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12274-021-3579-5.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green and orange emissive carbon dots with high quantum yields dispersed in matrices for phosphor-based white LEDs
ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng.
